summary: DHH highlights Collaboraid, a development firm that signed a major contract with a large NGO to build their intranet using Ruby on Rails. He emphasizes how quickly the firm was able to learn Ruby and Rails and secure commercial work with it. The post uses this as evidence against the argument that non-mainstream programming languages create barriers for development firms. DHH frames this as a victory over 'fear-driven technology' choices.
key_points: Collaboraid rapidly adopted Ruby on Rails and landed a significant commercial contract with a large NGO
The ease of learning Ruby and Rails — through documentation, community support, and direct mentorship — undermines claims that off-mainstream languages are risky business choices
DHH positions this as a counter-example to 'fear-driven technology' decisions that default to mainstream stacks
money_quotes: This shoots a big hole in the whole notion that off-mainstream languages are great barriers to entry for development firms.
It's wonderful to see development firms like Collaboraid trumph fear-driven technology choices and work with forward-thinking organizations to get working software into the hands of users faster.
key_insight: Early commercial adoption of Rails by development firms proved that choosing non-mainstream technology was a competitive advantage, not a business risk.
